comments.body: When you compare the pattern to the value, you should notice there's a difference.
It seems that your input is not a local date time, but one with an offset.

Apart from what @f1sh wrote: You have a String representation of a date with time and offset from UTC, but a LocalDateTime will not be able to store that offset. You should either switch to OffsetDateTime or parse and ignore the offset. Yes, the offset is one of the differences, but there's at least one more: fractions of second.
Take a look at Jackson Date. The simplest solution is to change LocalDateTime to OffsetDateTime - Jackson: parse custom offset date time
Please provide enough code so others can better understand or reproduce the problem.
@MichałZiober That is not only simpler, it is also the correct solution. LocalDateTime is the wrong class to use for the time when something was created.

Thanks for answering your own question. I am sorry, you don’t want this. You are deserializing 2023-01-13T08:54:25.83-12:00 and 2023-01-13T08:54:25.83+14:00 into the same LocalDateTime value even though they denote points in time that are 26 hours apart, more than a full day. You need the UTC offset from JSON or at least the point in time denoted in the JSON. LocalDateTime is the wrong class to use for that.
Let me summarize the Comment above: This Answer is incorrect. Given your input, use OffsetDateTime, not LocalDateTime.

answers.body:
Done, thank you.

@JsonDeserialize(using = LocalDateTimeDeserializer.class)
@JsonProperty("created_at")
private LocalDateTime createdAt;

...

public class LocalDateTimeDeserializer extends JsonDeserializer<LocalDateTime> {

@Override
    public LocalDateTime deserialize(JsonParser p, DeserializationContext ctxt) throws IOException, JsonProcessingException {
        return OffsetDateTime.parse( p.getText() ).toLocalDateTime();
    }

}

java.time.OffsetDateTime, not LocalDateTime

The Answer by Tavares is incorrect. That solution uses the class java.time.LocalDateTime. That type represents merely a date with time-of-day but lacks the context of a time zone or offset-from-UTC.

In contrast, the input text 2023-01-13T08:54:25.83-03:00 includes -03:00 on the end. That piece of text represents an offset-from-UTC. In this case the meaning is "8 in the morning as seen from three hours behind the temporal meridian of UTC". That offset is used by people in time zones such as Antarctica/Palmer and America/Buenos_Aires. So we need a class that represents three parts rather than two: date, time-of-day, and offset. That class would be java.time.OffsetDateTime.

Using OffsetDateTime with Jackson

Jackson unfortunately does not support that data type by default.

Fortunately, there is an additional Jackson library that can. So, in addition to the dependency configuration core Jackson library (here, Apache Maven POM file):

<dependency>
    <groupId>com.fasterxml.jackson.core</groupId>
    <artifactId>jackson-databind</artifactId>
    <version>2.20.0</version>
</dependency>

… we also need a dependency for jackson-datatype-jsr310. ("jsr310" refers to the java.time specification, JSR 310.)

<dependency>
    <groupId>com.fasterxml.jackson.datatype</groupId>
    <artifactId>jackson-datatype-jsr310</artifactId>
    <version>2.20.0</version>
</dependency>

For Gradle:

implementation 'com.fasterxml.jackson.core:jackson-databind:2.20.0'
implementation 'com.fasterxml.jackson.datatype:jackson-datatype-jsr310:2.20.0'

And if your Java project is modularized, add to your module-info.java:

    // Jackson typically resolves as 'com.fasterxml.jackson.databind'.
    // If your IDE/Maven shows a different automatic module name, use that.
    requires com.fasterxml.jackson.databind;
    requires com.fasterxml.jackson.datatype.jsr310;

    …

    // Allow reflective access for JSON serialization/deserialization
    opens work.basil.example.json to com.fasterxml.jackson.databind;
    opens work.basil.example.web to com.fasterxml.jackson.datatype.jsr310;

Unfortunately, Jackson, by default, normalizes OffsetDateTime to UTC (Z, offset of zero) during deserialization. To preserve the original offset, we need additional code. Furthermore, Jackson by default will truncate fractional seconds, so we need code for that. To solve these problems we must write our own classes to help Jackson with serializing & deserializing.

Stick these two subclasses somewhere in your codebase. If you choose to nest them, you can make them static.

    // ------|  Serializer  |------------------------
    // Custom serializer to preserve OffsetDateTime offset with fractional second.
    static class CustomOffsetDateTimeSerializer extends StdSerializer < OffsetDateTime >
    {
        private static final DateTimeFormatter FORMATTER =
                new DateTimeFormatterBuilder()
                        .appendPattern( "yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss" )
                        .appendFraction( java.time.temporal.ChronoField.NANO_OF_SECOND , 0 , 9 , true )
                        .appendPattern( "XXX" )
                        .toFormatter();

        public CustomOffsetDateTimeSerializer ( ) { this( null ); }

        public CustomOffsetDateTimeSerializer ( Class < OffsetDateTime > t ) { super( t ); }

        @Override
        public void serialize ( OffsetDateTime value , JsonGenerator gen , SerializerProvider provider ) throws IOException
        {
            gen.writeString( FORMATTER.format( value ) );
        }
    }

    // ------|  Deserializer  |------------------------
    // Custom deserializer to preserve OffsetDateTime offset with fractional second.
    static class CustomOffsetDateTimeDeserializer extends StdDeserializer < OffsetDateTime >
    {
        private static final DateTimeFormatter FORMATTER =
                new DateTimeFormatterBuilder()
                        .appendPattern( "yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss" )
                        .appendFraction( java.time.temporal.ChronoField.NANO_OF_SECOND , 0 , 9 , true )
                        .appendPattern( "XXX" )
                        .toFormatter();

        public CustomOffsetDateTimeDeserializer ( ) { this( null ); }

        public CustomOffsetDateTimeDeserializer ( Class < OffsetDateTime > t ) { super( t ); }

        @Override
        public OffsetDateTime deserialize ( final JsonParser p , final DeserializationContext context ) throws IOException
        {
            String date = p.getText();
            return OffsetDateTime.parse( date , FORMATTER );
        }
    }

Example code

Let's write a local record class as an example. We configure Jackson’s JavaTimeModule to use our custom serializer/deserializer classes. We go on to serialize as well as deserialize, comparing output to input.

record Event( String name , OffsetDateTime when ) { }

Event event = new Event( "Whatever" , OffsetDateTime.now() );
IO.println( "event = " + event );

JavaTimeModule module = new JavaTimeModule();
module.addSerializer( OffsetDateTime.class , new CustomOffsetDateTimeSerializer() );
module.addDeserializer( OffsetDateTime.class , new CustomOffsetDateTimeDeserializer() );

ObjectMapper mapper = new ObjectMapper();
mapper.registerModule( module );
mapper.disable( SerializationFeature.WRITE_DATES_AS_TIMESTAMPS );
try
{
    // Serialize Event to JSON
    String json = mapper.writeValueAsString( event );
    IO.println( "Serialized JSON: " + System.lineSeparator() + json );  // Before Java 25, swap `IO` out for `System.out`.

    // Deserialize JSON back to Event
    Event deserializedEvent = mapper.readValue( json , Event.class );
    boolean isEqual = event.equals( deserializedEvent );

    // Dump to console.
    IO.println( "Is deserialized event equal to original event? " + isEqual );
    IO.println( "Deserialized Event: " + deserializedEvent );
}
catch ( IOException e )
{
    e.printStackTrace();
}

When run:

event = Event[name=Whatever, when=2025-09-29T15:42:55.812577-07:00]
Serialized JSON: 
{"name":"Whatever","when":"2025-09-29T15:42:55.812577-07:00"}
Is deserialized event equal to original event? true
Deserialized Event: Event[name=Whatever, when=2025-09-29T15:42:55.812577-07:00]

By the way, FYI, the Java team is workin on revamping Java Serialization. See talk by Viktor Klang, summer of 2025, Marshalling: Data-Oriented Serialization.

title: Java Jackson Deserialize 2023-01-13T08:54:25.83-03:00
body:
How can I deserialize a LocalDateTime in this format 2023-01-13T08:54:25.83-03:00 using Jackson?

I am tryin to use annotations

@JsonFormat(pattern = "yyyy-MM-dd'T'hh:mm:ss", shape = JsonFormat.Shape.STRING)
@JsonProperty("created_at")
private LocalDateTime createdAt;
